rationale_for_baseline_tier: T2 (prompt GP review within 24-48 hours). This is a severe flare of KNOWN chronic plaque psoriasis precipitated by topical-steroid withdrawal and stress, with a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen on patient-obtainable data — afebrile (37.4 C), no visible sterile pustules, bilateral symmetric distribution, no unilateral hot spreading area. It needs prompt in-person review to arrange a dermatology referral and systemic therapy, plus topical rescue in the interim. It does NOT meet criteria for ED/000: the patient is haemodynamically stable and afebrile and the emergent mimics (generalized pustular psoriasis, cellulitis/sepsis) are currently screened negative. Extensive fissuring keeps the risk live, so mandatory time-bound safety-net triggers escalate to ED/000 if fever/rigors, pustulation, spreading hot erythema, or systemic compromise develop.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with rigors/shivering, or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with rigors on a background of extensive fissured psoriasis signals generalized pustular psoriasis with systemic upset or secondary sepsis/bacteraemia — a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate hospital care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a high fever with shivering or shakes, or start feeling really unwell in yourself, call 000 for an ambulance straight away — don't wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Skin suddenly develops widespread small pus-filled blisters / pustules
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department the same day / immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: Sudden widespread sterile pustulation indicates generalized pustular psoriasis — a dermatological emergency needing urgent in-person and specialist management; the steroid-withdrawal trigger makes this a live risk.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your skin suddenly breaks out in lots of small pus-filled blisters or pimple-like spots, go to the emergency department straight away — this needs urgent specialist care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: One area becomes hot, spreading, increasingly painful, red-streaked, or discharges pus
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day urgent assessment or ED — do not wait for the routine review
escalation_edges.rationale: A localised hot, unilateral, rapidly spreading area, streaking, or purulent discharge from a fissure indicates superimposed cellulitis/secondary infection through the barrier breach — needs urgent assessment and antibiotics before systemic spread.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one patch becomes hot, swollen and much more painful than the rest, starts spreading quickly, develops red streaks, or oozes pus — get seen urgently the same day or go to the ED.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep fluids down, feeling faint/dizzy, confused, or passing much less urine
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to ED the same day / immediately if severe
escalation_edges.rationale: Extensive barrier failure can cause fluid and heat loss; signs of dehydration or systemic compromise indicate the flare is causing systemic instability and needs emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep fluids down, feel faint or confused, or notice you're passing much less urine, go to the emergency department — you may be getting dehydrated and unwell from the widespread skin involvement.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement or continued worsening of plaques/pain despite topical rescue over 48-72 hours
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Bring the GP review forward to same-day/urgent
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure to respond to appropriate topical rescue indicates the flare needs earlier escalation of care and expedited systemic therapy, short of an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin and pain aren't improving, or are getting worse, over the next 2-3 days despite the creams, bring your GP appointment forward and be seen sooner.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Bedbound and reliant on spouse for hydration, hygiene and topical care tonight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the spouse can help apply treatment and monitor overnight, and lower the threshold to escalate given the patient cannot self-care or self-transport easily — ensure they know exactly which signs mean call 000.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult (age inferred, not stated in source)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Standard adult thresholds apply; reconfirm age and any comorbidities/medications at GP review before systemic therapy is chosen.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: For tonight you can manage this at home: after a cool (not hot) shower, apply your steroid ointment and seal it with a thick layer of plain white soft paraffin, wear loose cotton clothing and keep the room cool. Take paracetamol for the pain. Arrange to see your GP within the next 24-48 hours to organise a dermatology referral and discuss stronger (systemic) treatment — this flare is too severe for creams alone to fix long-term.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book with your regular GP within 24-48 hours; for after-hours advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84).
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours or weekends: call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for triage advice. For a high fever with rigors, a sudden pustular breakout, or a hot spreading area, call 000 or go to the nearest emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the GP when the flare started, that you ran out of your steroid cream about three days ago, your recent stress, and any fever or new pustules — and ask specifically about a dermatology referral and systemic treatment.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Advising pure self-care with no medical review misses the need for a dermatology/systemic pathway and removes the clinician safety-net for generalized pustular psoriasis and secondary sepsis.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The flare progresses to pustular or erythrodermic psoriasis or secondary sepsis unrecognised; barrier failure causes dehydration and deterioration at home with no escalation plan.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine 1-2 week GP review is too slow for a severe flare with extensive open fissures and secondary-infection risk that needs a prompt dermatology/systemic pathway.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an afebrile, haemodynamically stable psoriasis flare with a negative acute red-flag screen straight to ED/000 is over-triage — resource waste and patient distress. The correct action is prompt GP plus dermatology with robust safety-netting, reserving ED/000 for the defined escalation triggers.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine the skin to definitively exclude early generalized pustular psoriasis or superimposed cellulitis, or measure true BSA/severity and vital signs.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Rely on the patient-obtainable reassurance (afebrile, no visible pustules, bilateral symmetric distribution) to keep the baseline at T2 with prompt in-person review, while attaching explicit, mandatory, time-bound safety-net triggers that escalate to ED/000 if fever/rigors, pustulation, spreading hot erythema, or systemic compromise appear.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either reflexively escalating a stable afebrile flare to ED/000 because the skin cannot be examined (over-triage), or falsely reassuring the patient and omitting the generalized-pustular-psoriasis / secondary-sepsis safety-net (under-triage).
